immediate_action: Remove and destroy all infected leaves and fruit
Apply fungicide immediately (captan, myclobutanil, or mancozeb)
prevention: Prune trees for good air circulation
Apply dormant sprays in early spring
Rake and destroy fallen leaves in autumn
apple_scab.notes: Begin spray program at green tip stage and continue every 7–10 days during wet weather.

prevention: Monitor for early signs of disease
apple_healthy.notes: Plant looks healthy. Continue regular monitoring.

severity_indicators: bright orange spots on upper leaf surface
immediate_action: Apply fungicide at pink bud stage
Remove galls from nearby juniper/cedar trees
prevention: Remove alternate hosts (Eastern red cedar) within 1 km if possible
Apply preventive fungicide from bud break through June
apple_rust.notes: Rust requires two hosts (apple + juniper). Breaking the cycle on one host reduces pressure.

immediate_action: Avoid working with plants when wet
pepper_bacterial_spot.notes: Warm, wet conditions favor spread. Fixed copper programs work best when started preventively.

severity_indicators: lesions run parallel to leaf veins
premature leaf death in severe cases
immediate_action: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicide at first sign
corn_gray_leaf_spot.notes: Most economical to spray at VT/R1 growth stage when weather favors disease.

severity_indicators: rapid leaf death in severe cases
immediate_action: Apply fungicide (strobilurin or triazole) at early symptom stage
Remove severely blighted leaves if manageable
corn_leaf_blight.notes: Northern corn leaf blight: cigar-shaped lesions. Southern: smaller, more irregular.

severity_indicators: small brick-red to orange pustules on leaves
immediate_action: Scout at early pustule stage for best results
prevention: Early planting to escape peak spore pressure
corn_rust.notes: Southern rust is more aggressive than common rust. Act quickly when pustules appear before tasseling.

severity_indicators: white downy growth on leaf undersides
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Apply metalaxyl-based fungicide to remaining plants
Report to local agricultural extension if widespread
prevention: Improve drainage — disease favors wet soils
corn_downy_mildew.notes: Quarantine disease in some regions. Report unusual outbreaks to authorities.

corn_streak_virus.display_name: Corn Streak Virus / Rayure du Maïs
corn_streak_virus.pathogen: Maize streak virus (MSV) — transmitted by leafhoppers
severity_indicators: narrow yellow streaks along leaf veins
pale green to white stripes on young leaves
immediate_action: Remove and destroy heavily infected plants
Apply systemic insecticide to protect healthy plants
prevention: Early planting to avoid peak leafhopper populations
Control grassy weeds that harbor leafhoppers
Use reflective mulch to deter leafhoppers
corn_streak_virus.notes: No cure once infected. Resistant varieties are the most effective long-term solution.

corn_yellowing.pathogen: Multiple causes: nitrogen deficiency, waterlogging, virus, or fungal infection
severity_indicators: yellowing starting from leaf tips or margins
immediate_action: Check soil nitrogen — apply urea if deficient
corn_yellowing.notes: Yellowing from mid-rib outward suggests nitrogen deficiency. Yellowing from margins suggests potassium. Stripe patterns suggest virus.

severity_indicators: yellow to brown spots on leaves and midrib
dark brown spots on leaf sheaths
immediate_action: Apply fungicide if disease covers >50% of upper canopy before tasseling
prevention: Fungicide at V5–V8 in high-risk areas
corn_brown_spot.notes: Often confused with other leaf diseases. Key ID: brown midrib lesions are diagnostic.

severity_indicators: small round to oval yellow spots
immediate_action: Scout to confirm pathogen vs nutritional cause
Foliar micronutrient spray if deficiency suspected

corn_chlorotic_spot.pathogen: Nutritional deficiency or early viral infection
corn_chlorotic_spot.notes: Interveinal chlorosis on young leaves often indicates iron or manganese deficiency.

severity_indicators: purple to reddish coloration on leaves and stems
V-shaped purple patterns on older leaves
immediate_action: Check soil pH — low pH locks up phosphorus
Warm soil to improve phosphorus uptake
corn_purple_discoloration.notes: Cold soils commonly cause temporary phosphorus-related purpling that resolves as temperatures warm.

corn_insect_damage.pathogen: Various insects (stem borers, armyworms, aphids, etc.)
immediate_action: For stem borers: apply granular insecticide to whorl
For armyworms: apply pyrethroid at first sign
prevention: Early planting to avoid peak pest cycles
corn_insect_damage.notes: Correct insect ID is critical — treatment varies by pest species.

severity_indicators: large silvery-white to gray galls on ears, tassels, or leaves
galls eventually burst releasing black powdery spores
immediate_action: Remove galls before they burst (while silvery-white)
Dispose of galls away from field — do NOT compost
Bag and burn or bury deeply
recommended_products: No effective fungicide for established infection
Seed treatments help prevent systemic infections
corn_smut.notes: Corn smut (huitlacoche) is edible and considered a delicacy in Mexico. Galls can be harvested and sold if market exists.

corn_red_leaf.pathogen: Genetic trait, stress response, or potassium deficiency
severity_indicators: often at leaf margins or tips
associated with drought or heat stress
immediate_action: Check potassium levels — apply potash if deficient
prevention: Avoid moisture stress at critical stages
corn_red_leaf.notes: Some hybrids show red pigmentation naturally under stress — confirm with nutrient test.

cassava_mosaic.pathogen: Cassava mosaic begomoviruses (CMV family) — transmitted by whiteflies
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Do NOT use cuttings from infected plants
prevention: Control whiteflies with neonicotinoid seed/stem treatment
cassava_mosaic.notes: Most economically important cassava disease in Africa. Resistant varieties are the most effective and affordable solution.

severity_indicators: circular brown spots with yellow halos
cassava_brown_spot.notes: Usually not economically significant unless severe defoliation occurs.

severity_indicators: cankers on stems with gummy exudate
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected plant parts
Disinfect cutting tools between plants (10% bleach)
Avoid working in field when wet
cassava_bacterial_blight.notes: Spreads rapidly in wet conditions. Roguing infected plants early is critical.

immediate_action: Stake plants and improve air circulation
tomato_early_blight.notes: Most common tomato disease. Begin preventive spray when plants are 30 cm tall.

severity_indicators: small circular spots with dark borders and white/gray centers
tiny black dots (pycnidia) in spot centers
immediate_action: Apply mancozeb, chlorothalonil, or copper fungicide
prevention: Stake and prune for air circulation
tomato_septoria_spot.notes: Spreads rapidly in wet conditions. Key ID: tiny black pycnidia in center of spots.

prevention: Scout for early blight on lower leaves

immediate_action: Avoid working in field when wet
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Copper spray program starting at transplant
tomato_bacterial_spot.notes: Warm, humid conditions accelerate spread. Actigard can induce systemic resistance.

severity_indicators: large greasy water-soaked patches on leaves
white fuzzy sporulation on leaf undersides
dark lesions on fruit and stems
immediate_action: Remove and bag infected plant parts
prevention: Apply preventive copper or mancozeb sprays in wet weather
Scout at first sign of cool, wet weather
tomato_late_blight.notes: Can destroy entire crops in 7–10 days under favorable conditions. Act at first symptom — do not wait.

tomato_mosaic_virus.pathogen: Tomato mosaic virus / Tobacco mosaic virus (virus)
severity_indicators: mosaic pattern of light and dark green
immediate_action: Wash hands thoroughly after handling infected plants
Disinfect tools with 10% bleach or 70% ethanol
prevention: No smoking near tomato plants (tobacco carries TMV)
recommended_products: No cure — control vectors: Imidacloprid, Spinosad
tomato_mosaic_virus.notes: Highly stable virus — can survive on tools, clothes, and dried plant debris for years. Prevention is the only management.

tomato_yellow_leaf_curl.pathogen: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus — transmitted by silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci)
severity_indicators: upward curling and yellowing of leaves
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected plants if <20% of field
Apply systemic insecticide to control whiteflies
prevention: Use whitefly-proof insect screens in nursery
Avoid planting near older infected fields
tomato_yellow_leaf_curl.notes: One of the most devastating tomato diseases worldwide. Resistant varieties + whitefly control are essential.

severity_indicators: pale green/yellow spots on upper leaf surface
olive-green to brown velvety mold on lower surface
tomato_leaf_mold.notes: Primarily a greenhouse problem. Reducing humidity is the most effective control.

severity_indicators: fine stippling/bronzing on upper leaf surface
immediate_action: Wash plants with strong water spray to dislodge mites
Increase humidity — mites thrive in dry conditions
prevention: Monitor with sticky traps and magnifier
tomato_spider_mites.notes: Rotate miticide classes to prevent resistance. Never use the same active ingredient twice in a season.

severity_indicators: small dark brown spots often with yellow halo
tomato_brown_spot.notes: Often secondary to calcium deficiency or injury. Improve crop nutrition alongside fungicide application.

prevention: Monitor for downy and powdery mildew

severity_indicators: circular tan/brown leaf spots with black borders
prevention: Remove all mummies from vine and ground
Good canopy management for air circulation
Apply protectant fungicide before rain events
grape_black_rot.notes: Mummified berries are the primary inoculum source — their removal is critical for disease management.

prevention: Monitor for brown rot and leaf curl

severity_indicators: target-like concentric ring spots on lower leaves
potato_early_blight.notes: Weaken plants are more susceptible. Ensure adequate K and Ca nutrition.

immediate_action: Remove infected haulms before harvest if severe
prevention: Preventive copper sprays in wet weather
Destroy cull piles and volunteer plants
potato_late_blight.notes: Caused the Irish Famine (1845–1852). Can destroy 100% of crop in 7 days under ideal conditions for the pathogen.

prevention: Monitor for cane blight and anthracnose

severity_indicators: white powdery coating on leaf surfaces
yellowing and death of heavily infected leaves
immediate_action: Apply potassium bicarbonate or sulfur fungicide
squash_powdery_mildew.notes: Unlike most fungi, powdery mildew thrives in dry weather with high humidity. Milk spray is surprisingly effective organically.
